coredata.dc:title: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive sensing mercury in milk based on carbon quantum dots modified with europium (III) complexes
coredata.dc:creator: Gan, Ziyu
Hu, Xuetao
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
Zhang, Wen
Li, Yanxiao
Xu, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor was developed for Hg2+detection based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Europium (III, Eu3+) complexes. The CQDs modified with Eu3+ complexes displayed dual-emission wavelengths of 443 and 617 nm. The dual-emission fluorescence intensity was significantly increased in the presence of Hg2+. The developed sensor displayed excellent selectivity and sensitivity with the detection range of 1–20 nM and detection limit of 0.2 nM. With increasing Hg2+ concentrations, developed sensor showed clear and regular color alternations from strong blue, light blue, lavender to deep purple observed by the naked eyes. The dual-emission method was successfully used for visual and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in drinking water and milk samples with satisfied recovery from 97.6 % to 105.4 %. The sensor provides a generalizable platform for dual-emission fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on rare earth ions-modified CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photo-induced electron transfer
Hg2+
Lanthanide polymer
Milk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, Ad-Eu-DPA and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
Development of Hg2+ detection method using CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
Detection of Hg2+ in drinking water and milk
Optical properties of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
Detection principle of dual-emission fluorescence sensor
Development of Hg2+ detection method using CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
Investigation of selectivity for Hg2+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Technologies Research and Development Program
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis process of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA for highly selective detection of Hg2+.
TEM images of (a) CQDs (inset: particle size distribution and HRTEM image of CQDs), (b) Ad-Eu-DPA and (c) CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA.

(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of synthesized CQDs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs (black curve) and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA (red curve); (d) XPS spectra of CQDs (black curve) and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA (red curve) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, a fluorescent spectrum of CQDs and fluorescent spectrum of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA (Inset: from left to right are photographs of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA under the excitation of the 254 nm UV light lamp); (b) fluorescent lifetime decay curve of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA; (c) plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance of CQDs and quinine sulphate.

Emission spectra of (a) CQDs in the presence of Hg2+, (b) CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA in the presence of Hg2+ and (c) CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA using Eu3+ and CQDs in different volume ratios. The F617/F443 as a function of (d) different pH value and (e) different time. (f) The F617/F443 against incubation time of Hg2+ in CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA sensing system. The emission spectra were recorded at 275 nm excitation wavelength.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA in the presence of Hg2+ (0, 1, 2, 5, 8, 10, 12, 15 and 20 nM) (Inset: visual fluorescence images of dual-emission fluorescence sensor in the presence of various concentrations of Hg2+); (b) Linear fitting curve of mercury ion concentration (1∼ 20 nM) vs the sum of variation values of the two emission peaks (Δ1+Δ2).
Fluorescence response of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA to different metal ions. Inset is the corresponding fluorescence photograph of the sensor solutions under 254 nm UV lamp.
The comparison of the determination of Hg2+.
Determination of Hg2+ in drinking water and milk samples. Determination of Hg2+ in two samples. EX: 275 nm.

head.ce:title: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive sensing mercury in milk based on carbon quantum dots modified with europium (III) complexes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor was developed for Hg2+detection based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Europium (III, Eu3+) complexes. The CQDs modified with Eu3+ complexes displayed dual-emission wavelengths of 443 and 617 nm. The dual-emission fluorescence intensity was significantly increased in the presence of Hg2+. The developed sensor displayed excellent selectivity and sensitivity with the detection range of 1–20 nM and detection limit of 0.2 nM. With increasing Hg2+ concentrations, developed sensor showed clear and regular color alternations from strong blue, light blue, lavender to deep purple observed by the naked eyes. The dual-emission method was successfully used for visual and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in drinking water and milk samples with satisfied recovery from 97.6 % to 105.4 %. The sensor provides a generalizable platform for dual-emission fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on rare earth ions-modified CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are of the most dangerous pollutions in ecosystems [1]. Among them, mercury is extremely dangerous to our nervous and endocrine systems due to its unique properties such as non-biodegradable, higher mobility, good water solubility and severe biological toxicity [2,3]. The Hg2+ present in the environment even at low concentration can bioaccumulate in the food chain and result in damage to the kidneys and brain of humans [4,5]. Therefore, it is significantly necessary to develop a method for the detection of trace Hg2+ in food and environmental samples.

Several instrumental methods have been reported for Hg2+ detection, such as spectrophotometry [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [4], neutron activation analysis [7], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8]. The methods have been greatly influenced by the low turnaround time, complicated sample preparation, or expensive detection cost. Hence, rapid and low-cost detection of Hg2+ cannot be achieved. Fluorescence chemosensor has attracted a lot of attentions due to high sensitivity, high selectivity, rapid response, simple operation and low cost [9]. Previously, fluorescence chemosensors based on nanomaterials have been developed for Hg2+ detection [10,11]. Nevertheless, preparations of fluorescence chemosensors are often featured by complicated operation, expensive cost or high toxicity. Therefore, it is of paramount to explore a fast and low-cost method for preparation of fluorescence chemosensors.

The complexes formed by lanthanide ions and organic ligand have excellent fluorescence characteristics, but they have poor stability and biocompatibility. Therefore, lanthanide complexes combined with nanomaterials have been explored to form composites, overcoming the drawbacks of lanthanide complexes [12]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new sort of fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. It has been broadly utilized as fluorescence chemosensors for heavy metal ions detection [13]. Fluorescence chemosensors based on carbon quantum dots have a single emission peak, which could be readily disturbed by various factors resulting in low detection accuracy. The dual-emission fluorescence methods can overcome inherent problems and eliminate the interferences from a background with higher sensitivity [14]. Lanthanide functionalized carbon quantum dots have better fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, specificity or sensitivity, which have been previously reported to sense various species of biomarkers [15], hydrogen sulfide [16], antibiotics [17] and metal ions [18]. These documented results confirm the lanthanide functionalized carbon quantum dots are powerful analytical tool to sense various species. As a result, the functional combination of lanthanide functionalized carbon quantum dots and Hg2+ sensor has become a promising and hot research topic. However, most of the lanthanide functionalized carbon quantum dots only adopt one signal as readout with another signal remained unchanged, very few examples of dual-emission sensors were reported. Many efforts have been made to improve the stability and biocompatibility of lanthanide polymer [19]. However, very few studies can ensure that the materials among composites do not interfere with each other. Therefore, design, develop and assemble lanthanide functionalized carbon quantum dots remains a challenge to guarantee their own interference with each other.

In this work, a new strategy has been designed for ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ based on carbon quantum dots modified with europium complexes (CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA), CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA displayed dual-emission wavelengths by combining blue emissive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and red emissive europium complexes (Ad-Eu-DPA) [2]. The Ad-Eu-DPA was synthesized using adenine (Ad), dipicolinic acid (DPA) and europium ion (Eu3+). The citric acid and triethylenetetramine were used as raw material for the synthesis of CQDs. The rich amine and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs can form stable complexes with Ad-Eu-DPA. Based on the above points, current work expected to obtain a dual-emission fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection. Upon exposure to Hg2+, an obvious fluorescence enhancement of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA would be observed. According to the fluorescent enhancement of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA, the method for Hg2+ detection was developed.

Citric acid (≥99.8 %), triethylenetetramine (70 %, TETA), adenine (≥98 %, Ad), dipicolinic acid (99 %, DPA), mercury nitrate standard solution and metallic salts (e.g., NaCl, KCl, AlCl3, CaCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, MgCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, Pb(NO3)3, CrCl3) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company Limited, China. Europium nitrate (99.99 %, Eu3+) was purchased from Sigma-Aladdin Reagent, Shanghai, China. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ) was used for all the experiments. Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification.

The fluorescence signals were recorded using the F-98 spectrofluorometer (Lengguang Technology Company, China). The excitation wavelength was set at 275 nm and both slit widths were 10 nm for excitation and emission. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) spectrums were performed by JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). The morphologies of synthesized materials were observed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (EDAX, USA). The elemental compositions of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250, Thermo Scientific). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescent lifetime of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were investigated with QuantaMaster™ 40 Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometers (PTI, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a Rayleigh UV-1601 spectrophotometer (Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument Company. Limited, China). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using Bruker D8 advanced through Ni-filtered Cu Kα (λ =0.15406 nm). Raman spectrum of the CQDs was carried out using micro-Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw, United Kingdom) with a 633 nm laser excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, Ad-Eu-DPA and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs, Ad-Eu-DPA and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were presented in Scheme 1 . The CQD and Ad-Eu-DPA were synthesized according to the method reported previously with slight modifications [20,21]. Firstly, 1000 mg of citric acid and 2 mL of TETA were dissolved in 50 mL water and stirred for 5 min. The obtained solution was transferred into 100 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave followed by heating at 160 ℃ for 2 h. The CQDs was obtained by lyophilization. Secondly, 40 nM of adenine and 5 nM of DPA were transferred into 10 mL of DMF. Thereafter, 80 nM of europium nitrate was added into the above solution under continuous stirring. After stirring for 20 min, the obtained solution was transferred into 25 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 ℃ for 3 h. The Ad-Eu-DPA was obtained by freeze-drying.

Different volumes of CQDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) were added into 5 mL of an Ad-Eu-DPA (0.8 mg/mL) suspension. The total volume reached 20 mL by adding water and stirred for 20 min. Then the solutions were transferred into 50 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 ℃ for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the unreacted reactants were removed by dialyzing the white products with a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (500 MWCO) for 4 days. Finally, the obtained CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA (0.0324 g, dry) was mixed with 3 mL distilled water to form a suspension.

The quantum yield of CQDs was measured by the relative method with quinine sulfate (QS) as standard [22]. The QS (0.1 M H2SO4) and CQDs solution were prepared with the absorbance value of less than 0.1 under 368 nm. Thereafter, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and QS were determined under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The quantum yield (Φ) of CQDs was calculated by using the following equation:
Φx = (Фst (Gradx/Gradst) ((ηx 2)/(η2 st)))
Where Ф is the quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of fluorescence intensity vs absorbance and η is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for both solvents). The standard quantum yield of Φst is 54 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of Hg2+ detection method using CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The original CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA suspension was diluted 50 times to detect Hg2+. Then, 100 μL of Hg2+ solution (containing different concentrations) was added into 100 μL of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA suspension. After reacting for 6 min, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA at 443 and 617 nm were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 275 nm. To investigate the selectivity of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA to Hg2+, different interference ions fluorescence responses of the dual-emission fluorescence sensor were measured by following the same procedure mentioned above. Selected concentrations for interference measurement were 20 nM Hg2+, 20 μM of Al3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+ and Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in drinking water and milk
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the applicability of developed sensor, detection of Hg2+ was carried out in drinking water and milk sample. The drinking water and milk samples were purchased from a local market. Different volumes of Hg2+ standard solutions were added into drinking water for the preparation of water samples. Then, three water samples with different Hg2+ concentrations were added to 100 μL of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA suspension. After reacting for 6 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 275 nm.

Detection of Hg2+ in milk samples were carried out according to the previously reported method [5]. At first, 1 % (v/v) trichloroacetic acid was added into milk samples and sonicated for 20 min to remove the proteins. The samples were then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. Afterwards, the lipids were removed using supernatant filtered (0.22 mm membrane, Whatman). Different concentrations of Hg2+ solutions (5 nM, 10 nM, 15 nM) were added into the obtained milk samples. Subsequently, 100 μL of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA suspension was respectively added into these milk samples. The fluorescence spectra of resulting solutions were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 275 nm after 6 min of reaction time.

The morphology and size of the CQDs, Ad-Eu-DPA and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were explored using TEM images. Fig. 1 clearly shows that the CQDs, Ad-Eu-DPA and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were spherical nanoparticles. CQDs was well mono-dispersed and distributed in a range from 4.3 to 8.5 nm with an average size of 6.2 nm (Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM images (inset in Fig. 1a) revealed that CQDs possessed a loose crystalline structure, and the lattice fringe spacing was not clear. The average size of Ad-Eu-DPA decreased from 58 nm to approximately 39 nm in the presence of CQDs (Fig. 1b and c). This could be owing to Eu3+, which has replaced the part of the coordination positions of DPA and Ad.

A broad diffraction peak at 22.1° corresponding to the (002) plane of disordered carbons depicted a highly disordered structure of the fabricated CQDs (Fig. 2 a) [23]. The Raman spectrum indicated the presence of graphite nature in the CQDs (Fig. 2b). Two peaks at 1339 and 1571 cm−1 corresponded to the D band (sp3-hybridized) and G band (sp2-hybridized), respectively. The relative intensity ratio ID/IG value was 0.98, indicating the existence of defects in the prepared CQDs. FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2c, black curve) of CQDs displays the υC-H bands in the 2921 cm−1 regions, as well as the υCO, υCO and υOH/υNH bands at 1115, 1651 and 3446 cm−1, which confirmed the presence of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in CQDs [20]. These groups demonstrated that CQDs possessed excellent biocompatibility and water-solubility. The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA showed a slight change in the position of characteristic peaks when compared with CQDs. The band at 1397 cm−1 attributed to υCN, and the peak at 1583 cm−1 corresponded to υCO of carboxylate or δNH of amino groups [21,24–26]. The υCO, υCO and υCN intensities had a significance difference with CQDs, which suggested that the coordination of Ad-Eu-DPA polymer with CQDs’ carboxyl and amino groups changed the functional groups on the surface of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA.

Besides, the XPS and EDS spectra were used to explore surface elements and element distribution of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA. The XPS spectrum of CQDs showed three peaks at 285.1, 399.5 and 532.7 eV, which were associated with C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2d, black curve). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s demonstrated the presence of CN, CC, CN/CO and NH (Fig. S1a, b, c) [27,28]. Moreover, the relative atomic percentages of C, N and O elements in CQDs were 14.5, 0.75 and 85.2 %, respectively (Table S1). EDS was performed for chemical composition analysis on the surface of CQDs. Element mapping image (Fig. S2) indicated that CQDs was mainly composed of C, N and O elements and the uniform distribution of C, N, O on the surface of CQDs. These results revealed that CQDs was consisted of C, N and O elements and rich in amino and carboxyl groups on the surface. As seen in Fig. 2d (red curve), the prepared CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA contained Eu in addition to C, N and O elements (Fig. S1d). The relative atomic percentages of C, N, O and Eu were 37.45, 1.71, 49.14 and 11.7 % respectively. The atomic ratio of C, N, O, Eu of Ad-Eu-DPA was also obtained by mean of XPS analysis (Table S1). According to the atomic ratio of CQDs, Ad-Eu-DPA and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA, it was speculated that the average amount of chelated CQDs on every Ad-Eu-DPA was 0.383. Element mapping image in Fig. 3 also demonstrated that the CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA was mainly consisted of C, N, O and Eu elements, indicating that CQDs and Ad-Eu-DPA were successfully combined. All the characterization results indicated the successfully preparation of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
ce:section.ce:para:
Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum, fluorescence emission spectrum, fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were recorded to characterize optical properties. From the Fig. 4 a, CQDs showed two absorption peaks at 220 nm and 354 nm, which were attributable to the π -
π *
transition of CC and n-
π *
transition of CO [20]. Under the excitation of 250–310 nm light beam, the CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA showed excitation wavelength independent fluorescence emissions. As depicted in Fig. S3, the emission wavelength of 443 nm was attributed to CQDs and its fluorescence intensity increased with the excitation wavelength. Moreover, CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA also showed the three characteristic transition emission peaks of Eu3+ in Ad-Eu-DPA at 592, 617 and 692 nm, which were produced by 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2 and 5D0→7F4 transitions, respectively. However, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ increased with the excitation wavelength varying from 250 nm to 275 nm and then decreased to zero as the excitation wavelength increases. Therefore, 275 nm was established as an optimal excitation wavelength.

During the development of Ad-Eu-DPA, Eu3+ could coordinate with the N atom of the aromatic group and the O atom of carboxylic acid group in DPA. Meanwhile, Ad bond to DPA and Eu3+ through hydrogen bonding and N atom, respectively (Scheme 1) [29]. Lanthanide ions had low luminescent efficiency, but could emit strong fluorescence when binding to the ligand due to the antenna effect [30]. In the system of Ad-Eu-DPA, DPA was an ideal tridentate ligand for Eu3+, which could transfer absorbed energy to Eu3+, and eventually sensitizing its luminescent [31]. However, Ad-Eu-DPA had weak fluorescence intensity because the N atom of adenine transferred electrons to DPA, thus preventing the intra-molecular energy shift from DPA to Eu3+. The CQDs combined with Eu3+ via rich amino and carboxyl groups forming CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA composites. The luminescence of Eu3+ was enhanced due to the combination of CQDs and Eu3+, and this lead to reduce the quenching effect owing to the vibration of coordinated water molecules near Eu3+ [32].

Fig. 4b exhibits the fluorescence decay curve of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA excited at 337 nm. The average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA were measured to be 6.24 ns and 8.79 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA was increased because of CQDs bond with Eu3+ which decreases its non-radiative rate, and confirmed the Eu3+ was successfully combined with CQDs. The relative quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was calculated to be 28.6 % with quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. 4c), which was higher than other CQDs with blue fluorescence (Table S2). The high QY was because of the introduction of a new surface state by nitrogen doping [33]. All the characterization results showed that CQDs and CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA have excellent fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of dual-emission fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the excitation of 275 nm, the emission spectrum of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA consisted of two parts: the narrow broad emission wavelength at 443 nm due to CQDs and the sharp emission wavelength at 617 nm due to Eu3+. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the UV–vis spectrum of the CQDs showed an obvious new peak at 301 nm in the presence of Hg2+, which indicated the formation of a new complex. The presence of Hg2+ enhanced the fluorescence of CQDs (Fig. 5 a) because the bind of Hg2+ ions with the surface groups of CQDs hindered the rotation of surface groups and enhanced the π-conjugation, which reduced the non-radiative decay [34–36]. Luminescence enhancement was observed. Fig. 5b shows that the addition of Hg2+ could enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the two emission peaks (443 and 617 nm). Hg2+ with a higher affinity for N atoms could be covalently bonded to Ad and hindered the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process between Ad and DPA. Therefore, DPA could enhance the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 617 nm via transferring the absorbed energy to Eu3+.

The amounts of CQDs and Ad-Eu-DPA had an important influence for the synthesis of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA. Excessive CQDs with strong fluorescence intensity might cover the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm, resulting in single signal material. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA at 443 nm enhanced with increasing CQDs concentrations. However, the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm increased slowly as CQDs continued to increase. This may be because the binding site of Eu3+ has reached saturation leading to that extra CQDs cannot be combined with Eu3+. Therefore, Eu3+: CQDs volume ratio of 1: 1 was selected in current work to synthesize CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA.

The pH value of solution was considered as a critical factor for fluorescence response. As shown in Fig. 5d, the F617/F443 had no significant changes within pH 5–7, suggesting that CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA had better stability in acid environment. In addition, the water stability of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA was further explored by monitoring the F617/F443 value in different times. As could be seen from Fig. 5e, the fluorescence intensity had no significant change within 2 h, indicating that CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA had high water stability. The effect of incubation time of Hg2+ in the sensing system was measured to investigate the optimal reaction time of the CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA system to Hg2+. Fig. 5f shows that the F617/F443 significantly increased within 6 min once the CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA was exposed into the Hg2+ environment. After that, no obvious increase was observed with the incubation time, showing that the reaction equilibrium was rapidly reached. Thus, 6 min was set as an optimal incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of Hg2+ detection method using CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence response of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA to different concentrations of Hg2+ was shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA at 443 and 617 nm enhanced continuously with the increasing concentration of Hg2+ (Fig. 6a). The fluorescence intensity at 443 nm was higher than that at 617 nm initially. When the concentration of Hg2+ was 20 nM, the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm was higher than that at 443 nm, indicating the fluorescence intensity increase rate of Eu3+ was higher than that of CQDs. Clear and regular color alternations from strong blue, light blue, lavender to deep purple were monitored by naked eyes under the UV light at 254 nm (inset in Fig. 6a). Fig. 6b reveals a linear relationship between Hg2+ concentration and the sum of fluorescence intensity changes (Δ1+Δ2, Δ1=I ’
443-I443, Δ2=I ’
617-I617, I443 and I617 are the fluorescence intensities at 443 nm and 617 nm, respectively). The linear equation was Y = 8.5529X+ 12.5744 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9934. The linear detection range was from 1 nM to 20 nM. It can be clearly seen that the dual signal performs better than the single one (Fig. S5). In addition, the detection limit of 0.2 nM was lower than that reported previously (Table 1 ). The high sensitivity was because that one carbon dot which was rich in carboxyl and amine groups could combine with many Eu3+ ions. Besides, Hg2+ could effectively prevent the PET process, causing the valid energy transfer from DPA to Eu3+. The method satisfies the demands of Hg2+ analysis in food and environmental samples. The highest residual limit of mercury in drinking water was 10 nM reported by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of selectivity for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the selectivity of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA sensor, the fluorescence response towards various metal ions was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 7 , the sum of fluorescence intensity changes (Δ1+Δ2) of CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA to metal ions were nearly zero except Hg2+. Remarkable fluorescence color change occurred in the presence of Hg2+ and negligible changes occurred after adding other metal ions (inset in Fig. 7). The results showed that the sensor had good selectivity for Hg2+detection due to the high binding affinity between Hg2+ and adenine.

To evaluate the practicality of the sensing system, quantitative measurements of Hg2+ in drinking water and milk samples were carried out. The different concentrations of Hg2+ (5, 10, 15 nM) were detected in drinking water and milk samples using developed sensor (Table 2
). The recovery values of drinking water and milk samples were 98.5 %–105.4 % and 97.6 %–103.2 %, respectively. The relative standard deviation (n = 3) was recorded below 2.69 %. The results suggested that the dual-emission fluorescence sensor had high accuracy, great reproducibility and practicability for Hg2+ detection in environmental and food samples.

This work has successfully developed a dual-emission fluorescence method for Hg2+ detection based on the CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA. The CQDs@Ad-Eu-DPA exhibited two distinct characteristic emission wavelengths at 443 nm and 617 nm. The fluorescence sensor displayed high selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ detection with detection limit of 0.2 nM and detection range of 1–20 nM. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully employed for detection Hg2+ in drinking water and milk samples with satisfying recoveries (97.6 %–105.4 %). This method is suitable for ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ at low concentration. However, the preparation of composites needs to be further studied for more stable and excellent characteristics. Moreover, complexes of europium with different organic ligands will be further develop to extend the excitation wavelength.

Ziyu Gan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xuetao Hu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xiaowei Huang: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Zhihua Li: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jiyong Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wen Zhang: Formal analysis, Resources. Yanxiao Li: Validation, Investigation. Yiwei Xu: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31671844), National Key Technology Research and Development Program of China (2018YFD0400803, 2017YFC1600805) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128997.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A smart-phone-based electrochemical platform with programmable solid-state-microwave flow digestion for determination of heavy metals in liquid food

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, C. Liu, F. Liu, X. Zou, Y. Xu, X. Xu, A smart-phone-based electrochemical platform with programmable solid-state-microwave flow digestion for determination of heavy metals in liquid food, Food Chemistry, 303(2020) 125378.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of mercuric ion based on dye-doped lanthanide coordination polymer particles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhang, Y. Wu, S. He, Y. Xu, G. Li, B. Ye, Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of mercuric ion based on dye-doped lanthanide coordination polymer particles, Analytica chimica acta, 1014(2018) 85-90.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid ionic liquid-3D graphene-Ni foam for on-line preconcentration and separation of Hg species in water with atomic fluorescence spectrometry detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.C. Sotolongo, M.M. Messina, F.J. Ibañez, R.G. Wuilloud, Hybrid ionic liquid-3D graphene-Ni foam for on-line preconcentration and separation of Hg species in water with atomic fluorescence spectrometry detection, Talanta, (2019) 120614.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A modified fluorescein derivative with improved water-solubility for turn-on fluorescent determination of Hg2+ in aqueous and living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Guang, J. Tian, G. Wei, Z. Yan, H. Pan, J. Feng, et al., A modified fluorescein derivative with improved water-solubility for turn-on fluorescent determination of Hg2+ in aqueous and living cells, Talanta, 170(2017) 89-96.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide based dual-emission fluorescent probe for detection of mercury (II) in milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Tan, Q. Li, C. Ma, Y. Song, F. Xu, S. Chen, et al., Lanthanide based dual-emission fluorescent probe for detection of mercury (II) in milk, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 63(2015) 566-71.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupling laser ablation and atomic fluorescence spectrophotometry: an example using mercury analysis of small sections of fish scales

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Beaudin, S.C. Johannessen, R.W. Macdonald, Coupling Laser Ablation and Atomic Fluorescence Spectrophotometry: An Example Using Mercury Analysis of Small Sections of Fish Scales, Analytical Chemistry, 82(2010) 8785-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methods for the determination and speciation of mercury in natural waters—a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Leopold, M.E. Foulkes, P.J. Worsfold, Methods for the determination and speciation of mercury in natural waters—A review, Analytica Chimica Acta, 663(2010) 127-38.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe3+-catalyzed degradation of organic mercury as a simple post-column interface for the speciation of mercury by high-performance liquid chromatography-catalytic cold vapor-atomic fluorescence spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, D. Ji, Y. Zhang, Y. Lu, J. Fu, Z. Wang, Fe3+-catalyzed degradation of organic mercury as a simple post-column interface for the speciation of mercury by high-performance liquid chromatography-catalytic cold vapor-atomic fluorescence spectrometry, Journal of Analytical Atomic Spectrometry, 35(2020) 693-700.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Use of a smartphone for visual detection of melamine in milk based on Au@Carbon quantum dots nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Hu, J. Shi, Y. Shi, X. Zou, M. Arslan, W. Zhang, et al., Use of a smartphone for visual detection of melamine in milk based on Au@Carbon quantum dots nanocomposites, Food Chemistry, 272(2019) 58-65.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green anhydrous assembly of carbon dots via solar light irradiation and its multi-modal sensing performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Lu, Y. Tang, J. Gao, Y. Chen, Q. Wang, Green anhydrous assembly of carbon dots via solar light irradiation and its multi-modal sensing performance, Dyes and Pigments, 165(2019) 287-93.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signal transduction from small particles: sulfur nanodots featuring mercury sensing, cell entry mechanism and in vitro tracking performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Qiao, L. Liu, X. Hao, J. Zheng, W. Liu, J. Gao, et al., Signal transduction from small particles: Sulfur nanodots featuring mercury sensing, cell entry mechanism and in vitro tracking performance, Chemical Engineering Journal, 382(2020) 122907.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual lanthanide-doped complexes: the development of a time-resolved ratiometric fluorescent probe for anthrax biomarker and a paper-based visual sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, S. Xue, Z. Chen, S. Ma, S. Zhang, G. Shi, et al., Dual lanthanide-doped complexes: the development of a time-resolved ratiometric fluorescent probe for anthrax biomarker and a paper-based visual sensor, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 94(2017) 388-93.

Kang, et al., Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dithiothreitol modified carbon dots-gold nanoclusters for the sensitive detection of mercury ions in water samples, Sensors and Actuators B-chemical, 262(2018) 810-7.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Meng, Q. Xu, K. Zhao, Z. Li, J. Liang, Q. Li, A highly selective and sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for detecting Hg (II) based on Au/N-doped carbon quantum dots, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 255(2018) 657-65.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Pourreza, M. Ghomi, Green synthesized carbon quantum dots from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a dual off-on fluorescence probe for sensing mercury (II) and chemet drug, Materials Science and Engineering: C, 98(2019) 887-96.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new strategy to design colorful ratiometric probes and its application to fluorescent detection of Hg (II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Ghasemi, M.R. Hormozi-Nezhad, M. Mahmoudi, A new strategy to design colorful ratiometric probes and its application to fluorescent detection of Hg (II), Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 259(2018) 894-9.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Chen, J. Ma, T. Yang, L. Chen, P.F. Gao, C.Z. Huang, A portable RGB sensing gadget for sensitive detection of Hg2+ using cysteamine-capped QDs as fluorescence probe, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 98(2017) 36-40.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Daware, R. Shinde, R.S. Kalubarme, M. Kasture, A. Pandey, C. Terashima, et al., Development of optical sensing probe for Hg (II) ions detection in ground water using Au, Hexanedithiol and Rhodamine B nanocomposite system, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 265(2018) 547-55.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visual and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on a dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Xie, J. Dong, J. Duan, G.I.N. Waterhouse, J. Hou, S. Ai, Visual and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on a dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 259(2018) 1082-9.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaobo Zou is a Professor at the School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His researches mainly focus on rapid and nondestructive methods and equipment, including colorimetric sensors (e.g. gas sensors and sensor arrays), optical sensors (e.g. sensors based on fluorescence, Raman spectra and X-ray), electrical and electrochemical sensors, and infrared spectral and hyperspectral systems, for the detection of food agro-products quality, such as food freshness and maturity, food components distribution, food authenticity and food toxicant residues.

Jiyong Shi is a Professor at the School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. He has been concentrating on hyperspectral and multiple spectral systems for the detection of food components distribution and food authenticity over ten years. Currently, his research interests also include pigments and dyes based colorimetric sensors and nanomaterials based fluorescence sensors.
